revisions.content: #REDIRECT [[Bitcoin#The_block_chain]]

A [[Blockchain]] is a [[distributed data store]] that allows [[Transaction processing|transactions]] to be [[cryptography|cryptographically]] updated between machines running blockchain hosting and management software.

Developments from the first implementation, [[Bitcoin]], have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[Smart contract|smart contracts]].

The technology forms the basis of all [[cryptocurrency|cryptocurrencies]].

== Implementations ==
* [[Bitcoin#Block_chain|Bitcoin]] - [[Proof of work]]
* [[Namecoin]] - Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] - Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] - Additional [[Proof of stake]]

{{stub}}
{{Cryptocurrencies}}
[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]

A '''block chain''' is a [[distributed data store]]  that maintains a continuously growing list of data records that are hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]].  The most widely known application of a block chain is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[cryptocurrency|cryptocurrencies]], such as [[bitcoin]]. This record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines running the software.{{R|bc1}}

The technology forms the basis of all cryptocurrencies.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Temperton|first1=James|title=Bitcoin might fail but the blockchain is here to stay|url=http://www.wired.co.uk/news/archive/2014-11/24/bitcoin-myspace-cryptocurrency-blockchain|accessdate=22 May 2015|date=24 November 2014}}</ref>{{failed verification|date=May 2015}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a large number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and whose operators could be dishonest.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes, to determine with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and became final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks.  Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks are records that confirm when and in what sequence certain transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database.  Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of crypto-currencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends crypto-currency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis.  The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain.  In most crypto-currency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends currency units from a known valid wallet, and meets various other requirements such as including a sufficient miner "fee" and/or a certain time elapsed since the currency units were previously involved in a transaction.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the block chain.  In a crypto-currency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every node in a cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal , require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then broadcast these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

==Double spend solution==
Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[Smart contract|smart contracts]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>

== Implementations ==
* [[Bitcoin#Block chain|Bitcoin]] - [[Proof of work]]
* [[Namecoin]] - Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] - Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] - Additional [[Proof of stake]]

==References==
{{reflist|30em|refs=
<ref name="Antonopoulos2014">{{cite book | author = Andreas M. Antonopoulos | date = April 2014 | url = http://chimera.labs.oreilly.com/books/1234000001802/ch01.html | publisher =  O'Reilly Media | accessdate = 23 October 2014 | title = Mastering Bitcoin. Unlocking Digital Crypto-Currencies}}</ref>

<ref name=bc1>{{cite web|title=How does Bitcoin work?|url=https://bitcoin.org/en/how-it-works|accessdate=20 May 2015|publisher=Bitcoin.org}}</ref>

<ref name=kopstein>
{{cite news|author1=Joshua Kopstein|title=The Mission to Decentralize the Internet|url=http://www.newyorker.com/tech/elements/the-mission-to-decentralize-the-internet|accessdate=30 December 2014|publisher=The New Yorker|date=12 December 2013 |quote=The network’s "nodes"—users running the bitcoin software on their computers—collectively check the integrity of other nodes to ensure that no one spends the same coins twice. All transactions are published on a shared public ledger, called the "block chain"}}</ref>

<ref name="primer">{{cite web | url=http://mercatus.org/sites/default/files/Brito_BitcoinPrimer.pdf | title=Bitcoin: A Primer for Policymakers | publisher=George Mason University | work=Mercatus Center | year=2013 | accessdate=22 October 2013 | author=Jerry Brito and Andrea Castillo}}</ref>

}}

==Token-less blockchains==
There is an ongoing debate as to whether a bitcoin-like system with no native currency can still be considered a block chain.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Reutzel|first1=Bailey|title=A Very Public Conflict Over Private Blockchains|url=http://www.paymentssource.com/news/technology/a-very-public-confluct-over-private-blockchains-3021831-1.html|website=Payments Source|publisher=SourceMedia|ref=debate}}</ref> Some have argued that token-free blockchains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion_concurrency_control]] (MVCC) in databases.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Greenspan|first1=Gideon|title=Ending the bitcoin vs blockchain debate|url=http://www.multichain.com/blog/2015/07/bitcoin-vs-blockchain-debate/}}</ref>  Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a blockchain.

== Implementations ==
* [[Bitcoin#Block chain|Bitcoin]] – [[Proof of work]]
* [[Namecoin]] – Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Additional [[Proof of stake]]

== Implementations ==
* [[Bitcoin#Block chain|Bitcoin]] – [[Proof of work]]
* [http://digitalnote.org DigitalNote XDN] – [[Instant messaging]] on a chain, blockchain banking deposits based on [[Proof of work]]
* [[Namecoin]] – Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Additional [[Proof of stake]]
* [[Ethereum]] – Turing-complete smart contracts, 12 second block time

==Factory Banking==
Factory Banking is the creation of value via a series of transactions and services that happen simultaneously via an integrated value chain-shop-network configured platform. This value creation process is both cost-reductive and value-additive due to its integrated, immediate (or very fast) process and the potential that a supplier has to thus build in profit margin. It was developed by Daniel Mark Harrison<ref>{{cite av media|url=http://ftalphaville.ft.com/2015/08/10/2136954/that-de-la-lalalalala-bid-is-not-going-to-happen-right/|title=That De Lalalalala Bid|date=10 August 2015|work=FT.com|accessdate=2015-08-10}}</ref>, and is hypothesised to be the way in which value on the Internet of Things is configured. Factory Banking describes the way in which value is manufactured on a Blockchain via two or more processes which traditionally take place separately instead happening at once. The theory pre-supposes that there is more than one Blockchain, and that there are also multiple virtual and physical-world currencies.<ref>{{cite av media|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tBeVd-kaGB4|title=The Age of Factory Banking: What's A Bitcoin Worth?|date=14 May 2015|work=YouTube.com|accessdate=2015-06-12}}</ref>.

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a [[distributed database]]  that maintains a continuously growing list of data records that are hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]].  The most widely known application of a block chain is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[cryptocurrency|cryptocurrencies]] used in [[Bitcoin]]. This record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines running the software.{{R|bc1}}

The technology forms the basis of some cryptocurrencies, such as [[Bitcoin]], [[Ethereum]], [[Dogecoin]], and other [[AltCoin]]s.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Temperton|first1=James|title=Bitcoin might fail but the blockchain is here to stay|url=http://www.wired.co.uk/news/archive/2014-11/24/bitcoin-myspace-cryptocurrency-blockchain|accessdate=22 May 2015|date=24 November 2014}}</ref>

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:

* The ability for a large number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and whose operators could be dishonest.

* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.

* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).

* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.

* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.


A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks are records that confirm when and in what sequence certain transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends currency units from a known valid wallet, and meets various other requirements such as including a sufficient miner "fee" and/or a certain time elapsed since the currency units were previously involved in a transaction.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the block chain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal , require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

==Double spend solution==
Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>

==Token-less block chains==
There is an ongoing debate as to whether a bitcoin-like system with no native currency can still be considered a block chain.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Reutzel|first1=Bailey|title=A Very Public Conflict Over Private Blockchains|url=http://www.paymentssource.com/news/technology/a-very-public-confluct-over-private-blockchains-3021831-1.html|website=Payments Source|publisher=SourceMedia|ref=debate}}</ref> Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Greenspan|first1=Gideon|title=Ending the bitcoin vs blockchain debate|url=http://www.multichain.com/blog/2015/07/bitcoin-vs-blockchain-debate/}}</ref>  Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain.

== Implementations ==
* [[Bitcoin#Block chain|Bitcoin]] – [[Proof of work]]
* DigitalNote XDN – [[Instant messaging]] on a chain, blockchain banking deposits based on [[Proof of work]]{{cn|date=August 2015}}
* [[Namecoin]] – Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Additional [[Proof of stake]]
* [[Ethereum]] – Turing-complete smart contracts, 15 second block time

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. [https://namecoin.info/ Namecoin] for distributed domain name management, [http://swarm.fund Swarm] and [http://koinify.com Koinify] for decentralized crowd-funding, [http://www.synereo.com Synereo] for synchronous and asynchronous communication, [http://lazooz.org LaZooz] for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [http://ethereum.org Ethereum] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sector of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middlemen.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>

== Messages ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The Bitcoin blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the blockchain, so anyone who has the blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the blockchain, criticizing it as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the blockchain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s.

* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Additional [[Proof of stake]]
* [[Ethereum]] – [[Turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s, 15 second block time
* [[Proof of Existence]] – document verification

Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. [https://namecoin.info/ Namecoin] for distributed domain name management, [http://swarm.fund Swarm] and [http://koinify.com Koinify] for decentralized crowd-funding, [http://www.synereo.com Synereo] for synchronous and asynchronous communication, [http://lazooz.org LaZooz] for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [http://ethereum.org Ethereum] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sector of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>

==Token-less block chains==
There is an ongoing<ref>{{Cite web|title = It's All About the Blockchain - Money and State|url = http://moneyandstate.com/its-all-about-the-blockchain/|website = Money and State|accessdate = 2015-11-02|language = en-US}}</ref> debate<ref>{{Cite web|title = BitBeat: Blockchains Without Coins Stir Tensions in Bitcoin Community|url = http://blogs.wsj.com/moneybeat/2015/04/14/bitbeat-blockchains-without-coins-stir-tensions-in-bitcoin-community/|website = WSJ Blogs - MoneyBeat|date = 2015-04-14|accessdate = 2015-11-02|first = Michael J.|last = Casey}}</ref> as to whether a bitcoin-like system with no native currency, such as [https://erisindustries.com/components/erisdb/ ErisDB] (which is designed for enterprise use rather than as a cryptocurrency), can still be considered a blockchain.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Reutzel|first1=Bailey|title=A Very Public Conflict Over Private Blockchains|url=http://www.paymentssource.com/news/technology/a-very-public-confluct-over-private-blockchains-3021831-1.html|website=Payments Source|publisher=SourceMedia|ref=debate}}</ref> Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Greenspan|first1=Gideon|title=Ending the bitcoin vs blockchain debate|url=http://www.multichain.com/blog/2015/07/bitcoin-vs-blockchain-debate/}}</ref>  Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref> In either case, the use of blockchains in this way is very new (having only first been done in 2014).

== Messages ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The Bitcoin blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the blockchain, so anyone who has the blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the blockchain, criticizing it as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the blockchain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s.

* DigitalNote XDN – [[Instant messaging]] on a chain, blockchain banking deposits based on [[Proof of work]]{{cn|date=August 2015}}
* [[Namecoin]] – Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Additional [[Proof of stake]]
* [[Ethereum]] – [[Turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s, 15 second block time
* [https://erisindustries.com/components/erisdb/ ErisDB] – Blockchain without a native currency<ref>{{Cite web|title = BitBeat: Blockchains Without Coins Stir Tensions in Bitcoin Community|url = http://blogs.wsj.com/moneybeat/2015/04/14/bitbeat-blockchains-without-coins-stir-tensions-in-bitcoin-community/|website = WSJ Blogs - MoneyBeat|date = 2015-04-14|accessdate = 2015-11-02|first = Michael J.|last = Casey}}</ref> and with an access control/permissions layer. 
* [[Proof of Existence]] – document verification

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a [[distributed database]]  that maintains a continuously growing list of data records that are hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]].  The most widely known application of a block chain is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[cryptocurrency|cryptocurrencies]] originally used in [[bitcoin]].<ref>{{cite book|last1=Antonopoulos|first1=Andreas|title=Mastering Bitcoin - Unlocking Digital Cryptocurrencies|date=December 2014|publisher=O'Reilly Media|isbn=1-4493-7403-4|page=http://chimera.labs.oreilly.com/books/1234000001802/ch02.html|accessdate=3 November 2015|ref=http://chimera.labs.oreilly.com/books/1234000001802/ch02.html}}</ref> This record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store's nodes.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a large number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks are records that confirm when and in what sequence certain transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends currency units from a known valid wallet, and meets various other requirements such as including a sufficient miner "fee" and/or a certain time elapsed since the currency units were previously involved in a transaction.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the block chain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal , require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. [https://namecoin.info/ Namecoin] for distributed domain name management, [http://swarm.fund Swarm] and [http://koinify.com Koinify] for decentralized crowd-funding, [http://www.synereo.com Synereo] for synchronous and asynchronous communication, [http://lazooz.org LaZooz] for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [http://ethereum.org Ethereum] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sector of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and  most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]<ref>{{cite book|last1=Antonopoulos|first1=Andreas|title=Mastering Bitcoin - Unlocking Digital Cryptocurrencies|date=December 2014|publisher=O'Reilly Media|isbn=1-4493-7403-4|page=http://chimera.labs.oreilly.com/books/1234000001802/ch02.html|accessdate=3 November 2015|ref=http://chimera.labs.oreilly.com/books/1234000001802/ch02.html}}</ref> in separation of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains. Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}{{R|t3}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions (of digital signature chains) and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks are recorded that confirm when and in what sequence certain transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends currency units from a known valid wallet, and meets various other requirements such as including a sufficient miner "fee" and/or a certain time elapsed since the currency units were previously involved in a transaction.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, [[Swarm (cryptocurrency)|Swarm]] and [[Koinify]] for decentralized crowd-funding, [[Synereo]] for synchronous and asynchronous communication, [[LaZooz]] for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

==Token-less block chains==
There is an ongoing<ref>{{Cite web|title = It's All About the Blockchain - Money and State|url = http://moneyandstate.com/its-all-about-the-blockchain/|website = Money and State|accessdate = 2015-11-02|language = en}}</ref> debate{{R|t10}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain (such as ErisDB, an enterprise system criticized for propositions vulnerable to malicious exploitation and associating blockchain terminology for publicity{{R|t7}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}). Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref> In either case, the use of blockchains in this way is very new (having only first been done in 2014).{{Citation needed|date=November 2015}}

== Messages ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The Bitcoin blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the blockchain, so anyone who has the blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>

[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the blockchain, criticizing it as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the blockchain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s.

== Implementations ==
{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

==== Original Bitcoin protocol blockchain ====
Node Networks running software using the Bitcoin protocol and token.
* [[Bitcoin#Block chain|Bitcoin]] – [[Proof of work]]
* BitcoinXT – [[Proof of work]]

===== Sidechain implementations =====
Private or Public Node Networks (sidechains{{R|t5}}) that may or may not be based on the Bitcoin protocol for its specific processing or functionality and isolated from the Blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the Blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is of fully supported. These systems work in conjunction with the Blockchain.{{R|t5}}

* SideChain Elements{{R|t6}}{{unreliable source inline|date=November 2015}} - Product offering and project from blockchain explorer provider Blockstream 
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O Sidechain concept for identity

==== Alternative chains ====
Node Networks running software based on the Bitcoin protocol using alternative tokens (AltCoins), commonly referred to as AltChains, Alternative Chains,{{R|t11}}{{unreliable source inline|date=November 2015}} or Alternative Blockchains, and generally provide additional or and improvement of existing functionality of the original blockchain. 

* [[Namecoin]] – Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Additional [[Proof of stake]]
* [[Ethereum]] – [[Turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s, 15 second block time
* [[Proof of Existence]] – document verification

==== Controversial commercial concepts ====
Predominately in 2015 after increased venture capitol funding and financial institution interest, commercial products similar in concept but not based on the bitcoin protocol (some having no token of any kind) have been promoted using "blockchain" or "blockchain technology" terminology as a selling or marketing point.{{Citation needed|date=November 2015}}

* ErisDB – Blockchain without a native currency<ref>{{Cite web|title = BitBeat: Blockchains Without Coins Stir Tensions in Bitcoin Community|url = http://blogs.wsj.com/moneybeat/2015/04/14/bitbeat-blockchains-without-coins-stir-tensions-in-bitcoin-community/|website = WSJ Blogs - MoneyBeat|date = 2015-04-14|accessdate = 2015-11-02|first = Michael J.|last = Casey}}</ref> and with an access control/permissions layer. 
* Bankchain – itBit product offering not based on the Bitcoin protocol or any blockchain development

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a permissionless [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} and the inspiration of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains.{{R|t13}} Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction ouputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, Swarm and Koinify for decentralized crowd-funding, Synereo for synchronous and asynchronous communication, LaZooz for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

==Token-less blockchain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain (such as ErisDB, an enterprise system criticized for propositions vulnerable to malicious exploitation and associating blockchain terminology for publicity{{R|t7}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}). Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Messages ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the blockchain, so anyone who has the blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the blockchain, criticizing it as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the blockchain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s.

== Implementations ==
{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

=== Original bitcoin protocol blockchain ===
The bitcoin network uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

==== Sidechain implementations ====
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from Blockstream 
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity

=== Alternative chains ===
Networks running software based on the bitcoin protocol using alternative tokens (altcoins), commonly referred to as altchains{{R|t13}} or alternative blockchains, and generally provide additional or and improvd functionality of the original blockchain. Examples are:

* [[Namecoin]] – Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Adding [[proof of stake]] method
* [[Ethereum]] – [[Turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s, 15 second block time
* [[Proof of Existence]] – Document verification

=== Controversial commercial concepts ===
Predominately in 2015 after increased venture capital funding and financial institution interest, commercial products similar in concept but not based on the bitcoin protocol (some having no token of any kind) have been promoted using "blockchain" or "blockchain technology" terminology as a selling or marketing point.{{R|t14}}{{R|t15}}

* ErisDB – Alternative concept without a native token and with an access control/permissions layer. 
* Bankchain – itBit product offering not based on the bitcoin protocol or any blockchain development

* [[Ethereum]] – [[Turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s, 15 second block time
* [[Proof of Existence]] – Document verification
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - regulated blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental FIAT currencies

=== Controversial commercial concepts ===
Predominantly in 2015, after increased venture capital funding and financial institution interest, commercial products similar in concept but not based on the bitcoin protocol (some having no token of any kind) have been promoted using "blockchain" or "blockchain technology" terminology as a selling or marketing point.{{R|t14}}{{R|t15}}

* ErisDB – Alternative concept without a native token and with an access control/permissions layer. 
* Bankchain – itBit product offering not based on the bitcoin protocol or any blockchain development

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a [[distributed database]], the first of which was the [[bitcoin]] protocol,{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} and the inspiration of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains.{{R|t13}} Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, Swarm and Koinify for decentralized crowd-funding, Synereo for synchronous and asynchronous communication, LaZooz for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

==Token-less blockchain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain (such as ErisDB, an enterprise-optimised system{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}). Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Messages ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the blockchain, so anyone who has the blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the blockchain, criticizing it as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the blockchain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s.

== Implementations ==
{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

=== Original bitcoin protocol blockchain ===
The bitcoin network uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

==== Sidechain implementations ====
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from Blockstream 
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - SEC approved sidechain developed by Overstock

=== Alternative chains ===
Networks running software based on the bitcoin protocol using alternative tokens (altcoins), commonly referred to as altchains{{R|t13}} or alternative blockchains, and generally provide additional or improved functionality of the original blockchain. Examples are:

* [[Namecoin]] – Ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Adding [[proof of stake]] method
* [[Ethereum]] – [[Turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s, 15 second block time
* [[Proof of Existence]] – Document verification
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - regulated blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental FIAT currencies
* [https://erisindustries.com/components/erisdb/ ErisDB] – Blockchain design designed for enterprise use without a native token and with an access control/permissions layer.

'''Altchains'''

* [[Namecoin]] – Cryptocurrency merge-mined with Bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Cryptocurrency metaprotocol to Bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Cryptocurrency supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses on its blockchain, with a 15 second block time
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - Regulated "cryptocash" blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental FIAT currencies

'''Non-cryptocurrency databases'''

* [https://erisindustries.com/components/erisdb/ ErisDB] <ref>{{Cite web|title = Azure Blockchain as a Service Update #2 {{!}} Microsoft Azure Blog|url = https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/azure-baas-update-2/|website = azure.microsoft.com|accessdate = 2015-12-15}}</ref>– permissionable blockchain design designed for enterprise use without a native token and with an access control/permissions layer.
* [http://tendermint.com/ Tendermint] – open-source proof-of stake blockchain design. 
* [http://www.multichain.com/ Multichain]<ref>{{Cite web|title = Azure Blockchain as a Service Update #2 {{!}} Microsoft Azure Blog|url = https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/azure-baas-update-2/|website = azure.microsoft.com|accessdate = 2015-12-15}}</ref> – Tokenised permissioned blockchain design designed for enterprise use.
* [https://www.openchain.org/ OpenChain] - Tokenised distributed ledger based on the OpenAssets protocol.
