{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a [[distributed database]] that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} and the inspiration of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains.{{R|t13}} Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, Swarm and Koinify for decentralized crowd-funding, Synereo for synchronous and asynchronous communication, LaZooz for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

==Token-less blockchain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}. Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Messages ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the blockchain, so anyone who has the blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the blockchain, criticizing it as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the blockchain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s.

== Implementations ==
{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

=== Original bitcoin protocol blockchain ===
The bitcoin network uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

==== Sidechain implementations ====
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from [[Blockstream]]
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - SEC approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

=== Alternative chain designs ===
Networks running software which may be based on the bitcoin protocol but use either alternative cryptocurrency tokens (known as altcoins){{R|t13}} or entirely new implementations (which are simply database designs in the classical sense) both exist. These newer designs generally provide additional features or functionality which earlier cryptocurrency designs such as Bitcoin's did not. Notable designs include: 

'''Altcoins'''

* [[Namecoin]] – Cryptocurrency merge-mined with Bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Cryptocurrency metaprotocol to Bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Cryptocurrency supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses on its blockchain, with a 15 second block time
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - Regulated "cryptocash" blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental FIAT currencies

'''General-purpose databases'''

* ErisDB<ref>{{Cite web|title = Azure Blockchain as a Service Update #2 {{!}} Microsoft Azure Blog|url = https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/azure-baas-update-2/|website = azure.microsoft.com|accessdate = 2015-12-15}}</ref> – permissionable blockchain design designed for enterprise use without a native token and with an access control/permissions layer.
* OpenChain<ref>{{Cite web|title = Azure Blockchain as a Service Update #2 {{!}} Microsoft Azure Blog|url = https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/azure-baas-update-2/|website = azure.microsoft.com|accessdate = 2015-12-15}}</ref> - Tokenised distributed ledger based on the OpenAssets protocol.

{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

=== Original bitcoin protocol blockchain ===
The bitcoin network uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

==== Sidechain implementations ====
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from [[Blockstream]]
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - SEC approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

=== Alternative chain designs ===
Networks running software based on the bitcoin protocol but using alternative cryptocurrency tokens (known as altcoins){{R|t13}} or implementations which are simply database designs in the classical sense exist. These newer designs generally provide additional features or functionality which earlier cryptocurrency designs such as bitcoin's did not. Notable designs include: 

'''Altcoins'''

* [[Namecoin]] – Cryptocurrency merge-mined{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Cryptocurrency metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Cryptocurrency supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses on its blockchain, with a 15 second block time
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - Regulated "cryptocash" blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a [[distributed database]] that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The primary means by which this is accomplished is through the use of a timestamp server which, after batching recent valid transactions into "blocks," takes "a hash of a block of (database entries) to be timestamped and widely publishing the hash... (which) proves that the data must have existed at the time." The timestamp of each block includes the prior timestamp, "forming a chain, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,"{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.  

Other distinguishing characteristics of blockchains include various means to achieve consensus across their networks, such as [[Proof-of-work system|proof of work]] or [[Proof-of-stake|proof of stake]], as well as controlling write permissions to the blockchain through the use of cryptographic [[Digital Signature Algorithm|digital signatures]]. Current thinking in the space is that these characteristics, working in concert, allow a blockchain to serve as low-cost data infrastructure for transactions and other communications.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Global Finance Magazine - Blockchain – a disruptive force for good?|url = https://www.gfmag.com/topics/technology/blockchain-disruptive-force-good|website = Global Finance Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> 

The initial and most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} and the inspiration of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains (if a cryptocurrency){{R|t13}} or (if general-purpose, or built for specific non-cryptocurrency applications) "blockchain 2.0."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Block Chain 2.0: The Renaissance of Money|url = http://www.wired.com/insights/2015/01/block-chain-2-0/|website = WIRED|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+WIRED|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, Swarm and Koinify for decentralized crowd-funding, Synereo for synchronous and asynchronous communication, LaZooz for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

==Token-less blockchain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}. Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Messages ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the blockchain, so anyone who has the blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the blockchain, criticizing it as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the blockchain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s.

(which) proves that the data must have existed at the time." The timestamp of each block includes the prior timestamp, "forming a chain, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,"{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.  

Other distinguishing characteristics of blockchains include various means to achieve consensus across their networks, such as [[Proof-of-work system|proof of work]], [[Proof-of-stake|proof of stake]],<ref>{{Cite web|title = What Proof of Stake Is And Why It Matters|url = https://bitcoinmagazine.com/articles/what-proof-of-stake-is-and-why-it-matters-1377531463|website = Bitcoin Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> or [[Byzantine fault tolerance|PBFT]]<ref>{{Cite web|title = Tendermint vs PBFT|url = http://tendermint.com/posts/tendermint-vs-pbft/|website = tendermint.com|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>, as well as controlling write permissions to the blockchain through the use of cryptographic [[Digital Signature Algorithm|digital signatures]].<ref>{{Cite web|title = How do digital signatures in Bitcoin work?|url = https://www.cryptocompare.com/wallets/guides/how-do-digital-signatures-in-bitcoin-work/|website = CryptoCompare|publisher = https://plus.google.com/115435853043353159139|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> Current thinking in the space is that these characteristics, working in concert, allow a blockchain to serve as low-cost data infrastructure for transactions and other communications.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Global Finance Magazine - Blockchain – a disruptive force for good?|url = https://www.gfmag.com/topics/technology/blockchain-disruptive-force-good|website = Global Finance Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> 

The initial and most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} and the inspiration of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains (if a cryptocurrency){{R|t13}} or (if general-purpose, or built for specific non-cryptocurrency applications) "blockchain 2.0."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Block Chain 2.0: The Renaissance of Money|url = http://www.wired.com/insights/2015/01/block-chain-2-0/|website = WIRED|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+WIRED|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs. In other systems (such as [[Ethereum]] or other smart contract systems<ref>{{Cite web|title = Analysis of Data Structures on the Ethereum Blockchain/Current draft - cwi.unik.no|url = http://cwi.unik.no/wiki/Analysis_of_Data_Structures_on_the_Ethereum_Blockchain/Current_draft|website = cwi.unik.no|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>), however, [[Stored procedure|stored procedures]] are uploaded to the blockchain at specified addresses,<ref>{{Cite web|title = Delivery versus payment on a blockchain {{!}} MultiChain|url = http://www.multichain.com/blog/2015/09/delivery-versus-payment-blockchain/|website = www.multichain.com|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> generally referred to as "accounts,"<ref>{{Cite web|title = Analysis of Data Structures on the Ethereum Blockchain/Current draft - cwi.unik.no|url = http://cwi.unik.no/wiki/Analysis_of_Data_Structures_on_the_Ethereum_Blockchain/Current_draft|website = cwi.unik.no|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> and it is these accounts and their associated state which controls who can write to the database and how.

Meanwhile, miners (or, in the case of permissioned systems, pre-appointed transaction validators) attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction. In a non-decentralised system such as Eris,<ref name=":0">Swanson, Tim. "[http://www.ofnumbers.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Permissioned-distributed-ledgers.pdf Consensus-as-a-service: a brief report on the emergence of permissioned, distributed ledger systems]." Published 6 August 2015, accessed 15 December 2015. </ref> Blockstream's "federated peg" sidechains<ref name=":0">Swanson, Tim. "[http://www.ofnumbers.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Permissioned-distributed-ledgers.pdf Consensus-as-a-service: a brief report on the emergence of permissioned, distributed ledger systems]." Published 6 August 2015, accessed 15 December 2015. </ref> or MultiChain,<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title = Dr Gideon Greenspan: Blockchain design is academic work and shouldn't just be decided by banks|url = http://www.ibtimes.co.uk/dr-gideon-greenspan-blockchain-design-academic-work-shouldnt-just-be-decided-by-banks-1520754|website = International Business Times UK|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+IBTimesUK/posts|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> appending a block is dependent on one authorised validator, or a quorum of the validator set, signing that block with their private keys.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Explainer {{!}} Permissioned Blockchains|url = https://docs.erisindustries.com/explainers/permissioned_blockchains/|website = Eris Industries Documentation|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>

==Peer-to-peer network architecture==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a blockchain network has a complete or partial copy of the block chain, allowing a blockchain to be run as a [[peer-to-peer]] application.. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require where the [[Master/slave (technology)|master/slave]] model dictates the relationship between the user and the provider of the application.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending;{{R|t3}} other blockchains store other types of data, e.g. one common approach being to record state transitions of smart contracts.<ref>{{Cite web|title = ethereum/wiki|url = https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/White-Paper|website = GitHub|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> Blockchains use various timestamping schemes, including [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency{{r|kopstein}} or otherwise writing an unauthorised transaction to the blockchain in question.

==Developments==
Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, Swarm and Koinify for decentralized crowd-funding, Synereo for synchronous and asynchronous communication, LaZooz for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

==Token-less blockchain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain system without a native token should still be called a blockchain{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}. Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Messages ==
The bitcoin blockchain can perform a number of functions other than value transfer, most of which any other blockchain design can replicate to a certain extent. For example, a blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various third party application services can store messages directly in a blockchain, so anyone who has a copy of that blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the Bitcoin blockchain, in particular, criticizing the practice as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications such as Factom are designed to store a hash value in the blockchain, demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s. Such functionality is seen in the Counterparty and OpenAssets protocols on top of Bitcoin, and numerous new chain designs which have been built from scratch. 

== Implementations ==
{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

=== Original bitcoin protocol blockchain ===
The bitcoin network uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

==== Sidechain implementations ====
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from [[Blockstream]]
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - SEC approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

=== Newer chain designs ===
Networks running software based on the bitcoin protocol but using alternative cryptocurrency tokens (known as altcoins){{R|t13}} or implementations which are simply database designs in the classical sense exist. These newer designs generally provide additional features or functionality which earlier cryptocurrency designs such as bitcoin's did not. Notable designs include: 

'''Altcoins'''

* [[Namecoin]] – Cryptocurrency merge-mined{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Cryptocurrency metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Cryptocurrency supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses on its blockchain, with a 15 second block time
"'''Blockchain 2.0'''" platforms

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a [[distributed database]] that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The primary means by which this is accomplished is through the use of a timestamp server which, after batching recent valid transactions into "blocks," takes "a hash of a block of (database entries) to be timestamped and widely publishing the hash... (which) proves that the data must have existed at the time." The timestamp of each block includes the prior timestamp, "forming a chain, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,"{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.  

Other distinguishing characteristics of blockchains include various means to achieve consensus across their networks, such as [[Proof-of-work system|proof of work]], [[Proof-of-stake|proof of stake]],<ref>{{Cite web|title = What Proof of Stake Is And Why It Matters|url = https://bitcoinmagazine.com/articles/what-proof-of-stake-is-and-why-it-matters-1377531463|website = Bitcoin Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> or [[Byzantine fault tolerance|PBFT]]<ref>{{Cite web|title = Tendermint vs PBFT|url = http://tendermint.com/posts/tendermint-vs-pbft/|website = tendermint.com|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>, as well as controlling write permissions to the blockchain through the use of cryptographic [[Digital Signature Algorithm|digital signatures]].<ref>{{Cite web|title = How do digital signatures in Bitcoin work?|url = https://www.cryptocompare.com/wallets/guides/how-do-digital-signatures-in-bitcoin-work/|website = CryptoCompare|publisher = https://plus.google.com/115435853043353159139|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>  

The initial and most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} and the inspiration of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains (if a cryptocurrency){{R|t13}} or (if general-purpose, or built for specific non-cryptocurrency applications) "blockchain 2.0."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Block Chain 2.0: The Renaissance of Money|url = http://www.wired.com/insights/2015/01/block-chain-2-0/|website = WIRED|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+WIRED|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}

Current thinking in the space is that these characteristics, working in concert, allow a blockchain to serve as low-cost data infrastructure for transactions and other communications.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Global Finance Magazine - Blockchain – a disruptive force for good?|url = https://www.gfmag.com/topics/technology/blockchain-disruptive-force-good|website = Global Finance Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> A number of the world's pre-eminent software businesses, including [[IBM]], [[Cisco Systems|Cisco]], [[VMware|VMWare]], and the [[Linux Foundation]], <ref>{{Cite web|title = IBM Creates Open-Source Blockchain With Linux and Big Banks - CoinDesk|url = http://www.coindesk.com/ibm-launches-open-source-blockchain-project-backed-by-linux-and-big-banks/|website = CoinDesk|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+Coindesk/|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> as well as many of the [[Bulge bracket|world's largest banks]],<ref>{{Cite web|title = 12 More Banks Join Blockchain Consortium R3|url = http://www.coindesk.com/twelve-banks-blockchain-consortium-r3/|website = CoinDesk|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+Coindesk/|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> have announced collaborations to develop "permissioned" blockchains and applications thereon for enterprise use (in relation to which a number of open-source designs are already in existence and in use).

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A Bitcoin-style block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs. 

In other systems (such as [[Ethereum]]), a third type of record - known as a "smart contract" - can also be stored on the chain. Smart contracts are [[Stored procedure|stored procedures]]<ref>{{Cite web|title = Analysis of Data Structures on the Ethereum Blockchain/Current draft - cwi.unik.no|url = http://cwi.unik.no/wiki/Analysis_of_Data_Structures_on_the_Ethereum_Blockchain/Current_draft|website = cwi.unik.no|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> uploaded to the blockchain at specified addresses,<ref>{{Cite web|title = Delivery versus payment on a blockchain {{!}} MultiChain|url = http://www.multichain.com/blog/2015/09/delivery-versus-payment-blockchain/|website = www.multichain.com|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> generally referred to as "accounts,"<ref>{{Cite web|title = Analysis of Data Structures on the Ethereum Blockchain/Current draft - cwi.unik.no|url = http://cwi.unik.no/wiki/Analysis_of_Data_Structures_on_the_Ethereum_Blockchain/Current_draft|website = cwi.unik.no|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> and it is these accounts and their associated state which controls who can write to the database and how.

Meanwhile, miners (or, in the case of permissioned systems, pre-appointed transaction validators) attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction. In a non-decentralised system such as Eris,<ref name=":0">Swanson, Tim. "[http://www.ofnumbers.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Permissioned-distributed-ledgers.pdf Consensus-as-a-service: a brief report on the emergence of permissioned, distributed ledger systems]." Published 6 August 2015, accessed 15 December 2015. </ref> Blockstream's "federated peg" sidechains<ref name=":0">Swanson, Tim. "[http://www.ofnumbers.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Permissioned-distributed-ledgers.pdf Consensus-as-a-service: a brief report on the emergence of permissioned, distributed ledger systems]." Published 6 August 2015, accessed 15 December 2015. </ref> or MultiChain,<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title = Dr Gideon Greenspan: Blockchain design is academic work and shouldn't just be decided by banks|url = http://www.ibtimes.co.uk/dr-gideon-greenspan-blockchain-design-academic-work-shouldnt-just-be-decided-by-banks-1520754|website = International Business Times UK|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+IBTimesUK/posts|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> appending a block is dependent on one authorised validator, or a quorum of the validator set, signing that block with their private keys.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Explainer {{!}} Permissioned Blockchains|url = https://docs.erisindustries.com/explainers/permissioned_blockchains/|website = Eris Industries Documentation|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>

==Peer-to-peer network architecture==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a blockchain network has a complete or partial copy of the block chain, allowing a blockchain to be run as a [[peer-to-peer]] application.. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require where the [[Master/slave (technology)|master/slave]] model dictates the relationship between the user and the provider of the application.{{r|"primer"}} 

With cryptocurrencies, transactions take the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' and are broadcast to this network using protocol implementations, often with several implementations existing concurrently for one blockchain (e.g. BTCD, BitcoinXT, BitcoinJ). Network nodes can then validate these transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and  [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending;{{R|t3}} other blockchains use them to timestamp other types of data, e.g. state transitions of smart contracts.<ref>{{Cite web|title = ethereum/wiki|url = https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/White-Paper|website = GitHub|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> Blockchains use various timestamping schemes, including [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency{{r|kopstein}} or otherwise writing an unauthorised transaction to the blockchain in question.

==Developments==
With cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin, the meaning of the data (Bitcoins) is ''intrinsic'' to the blockchain; that is to say, whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}} With non-cryptocurrency types, however, blockchains act simply as a communications system and a shared record between the various participants of the network.

Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, Swarm and Koinify for decentralized crowd-funding, Synereo for synchronous and asynchronous communication, LaZooz for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

==Permissioned blockchain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain system without a native token - which thus requires an access control layer to run and secure - should still be called a blockchain{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}. Most of the well-publicised newer blockchain implementations for enterprise use follow or aim towards this paradigm.<ref>{{Cite web|title = IBM Creates Open-Source Blockchain With Linux and Big Banks - CoinDesk|url = http://www.coindesk.com/ibm-launches-open-source-blockchain-project-backed-by-linux-and-big-banks/|website = CoinDesk|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+Coindesk/|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref>  

Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Messages ==
The bitcoin blockchain can perform a number of functions other than value transfer, most of which any other blockchain design can replicate to a certain extent. For example, a blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various third party application services can store messages directly in a blockchain, so anyone who has a copy of that blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the Bitcoin blockchain, in particular, criticizing the practice as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications such as Factom are designed to store a hash value in the blockchain, demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s. Such functionality is seen in the Counterparty and OpenAssets protocols on top of Bitcoin, and numerous new chain designs which have been built from scratch. 

== Implementations ==
{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

=== Original bitcoin protocol blockchain ===
The bitcoin network uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

==== Sidechain implementations ====
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from [[Blockstream]]
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - SEC approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

=== Newer chain designs ===
Networks running software based on the bitcoin protocol but using alternative cryptocurrency tokens (known as altcoins){{R|t13}} or implementations which are simply database designs in the classical sense exist. These newer designs generally provide additional features or functionality which earlier cryptocurrency designs such as bitcoin's did not. Notable designs include: 

'''Altcoins'''

* [[Namecoin]] – Cryptocurrency merge-mined{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Cryptocurrency metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Cryptocurrency supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses on its blockchain, with a 15 second block time
"'''Blockchain 2.0'''" '''platforms'''

[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a [[distributed database]] that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The primary means by which this is accomplished is through the use of a timestamp server which, after batching recent valid transactions into "blocks," takes "a hash of a block of (database entries) to be timestamped and widely publishing the hash... (which) proves that the data must have existed at the time." The timestamp of each block includes the prior timestamp, "forming a chain, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,"{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.  

Other distinguishing characteristics of blockchains include various means to achieve consensus across their networks, such as [[Proof-of-work system|proof of work]], [[Proof-of-stake|proof of stake]],<ref>{{Cite web|title = What Proof of Stake Is And Why It Matters|url = https://bitcoinmagazine.com/articles/what-proof-of-stake-is-and-why-it-matters-1377531463|website = Bitcoin Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> or [[Byzantine fault tolerance|PBFT]]<ref>{{Cite web|title = Tendermint vs PBFT|url = http://tendermint.com/posts/tendermint-vs-pbft/|website = tendermint.com|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>, as well as controlling write permissions to the blockchain through the use of cryptographic [[Digital Signature Algorithm|digital signatures]].<ref>{{Cite web|title = How do digital signatures in Bitcoin work?|url = https://www.cryptocompare.com/wallets/guides/how-do-digital-signatures-in-bitcoin-work/|website = CryptoCompare|publisher = https://plus.google.com/115435853043353159139|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> Each blockchain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes.{{R|bc1}}
==Applications==
The initial and most widely known application of the blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} and the inspiration of similar distributed ledgers known as altchains (if a cryptocurrency){{R|t13}} or (if general-purpose, or built for specific non-cryptocurrency applications) "blockchain 2.0."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Block Chain 2.0: The Renaissance of Money|url = http://www.wired.com/insights/2015/01/block-chain-2-0/|website = WIRED|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+WIRED|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> 

Current thinking in the space is that a blockchain's timestamp function, working in concert with an appropriate consensus mechanism and digital signature scheme, will allow them to serve as low-cost data infrastructure for transactions and other communications.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Global Finance Magazine - Blockchain – a disruptive force for good?|url = https://www.gfmag.com/topics/technology/blockchain-disruptive-force-good|website = Global Finance Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> A number of the world's pre-eminent software businesses, including [[IBM]], [[Cisco Systems|Cisco]], [[VMware|VMWare]], and the [[Linux Foundation]], <ref>{{Cite web|title = IBM Creates Open-Source Blockchain With Linux and Big Banks - CoinDesk|url = http://www.coindesk.com/ibm-launches-open-source-blockchain-project-backed-by-linux-and-big-banks/|website = CoinDesk|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+Coindesk/|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> as well as many of the [[Bulge bracket|world's largest banks]],<ref>{{Cite web|title = 12 More Banks Join Blockchain Consortium R3|url = http://www.coindesk.com/twelve-banks-blockchain-consortium-r3/|website = CoinDesk|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+Coindesk/|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref> have announced collaborations to develop blockchains with an access control layer (sometimes called "permissioned" blockchains)<ref name=":0">Swanson, Tim. "[http://www.ofnumbers.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Permissioned-distributed-ledgers.pdf Consensus-as-a-service: a brief report on the emergence of permissioned, distributed ledger systems]." Published 6 August 2015, accessed 15 December 2015. </ref> and applications thereon for enterprise use (in relation to which a number of open-source designs are already in existence and in use).

==Basic principles==
{{unreferenced section|date=July 2015}}
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single consensus of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious.
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set.
* The ability for any node that creates a transaction to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that conflicting transactions (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A Bitcoin-style block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became journaled as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business (in the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another), and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs. 

In other systems (such as [[Ethereum]]), a third type of record - known as a "smart contract" - can also be stored on the chain. Smart contracts are [[Stored procedure|stored procedures]]<ref>{{Cite web|title = Analysis of Data Structures on the Ethereum Blockchain/Current draft - cwi.unik.no|url = http://cwi.unik.no/wiki/Analysis_of_Data_Structures_on_the_Ethereum_Blockchain/Current_draft|website = cwi.unik.no|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> uploaded to the blockchain at specified addresses,<ref>{{Cite web|title = Delivery versus payment on a blockchain {{!}} MultiChain|url = http://www.multichain.com/blog/2015/09/delivery-versus-payment-blockchain/|website = www.multichain.com|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> generally referred to as "accounts,"<ref>{{Cite web|title = Analysis of Data Structures on the Ethereum Blockchain/Current draft - cwi.unik.no|url = http://cwi.unik.no/wiki/Analysis_of_Data_Structures_on_the_Ethereum_Blockchain/Current_draft|website = cwi.unik.no|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> and it is these accounts and their associated state which controls who can write to the database and how.

Meanwhile, miners (or, in the case of permissioned systems, pre-appointed transaction validators) attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction. In a non-decentralised system such as Eris,<ref name=":0">Swanson, Tim. "[http://www.ofnumbers.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Permissioned-distributed-ledgers.pdf Consensus-as-a-service: a brief report on the emergence of permissioned, distributed ledger systems]." Published 6 August 2015, accessed 15 December 2015. </ref> Blockstream's "federated peg" sidechains<ref name=":0">Swanson, Tim. "[http://www.ofnumbers.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/04/Permissioned-distributed-ledgers.pdf Consensus-as-a-service: a brief report on the emergence of permissioned, distributed ledger systems]." Published 6 August 2015, accessed 15 December 2015. </ref> or MultiChain,<ref name=":1">{{Cite web|title = Dr Gideon Greenspan: Blockchain design is academic work and shouldn't just be decided by banks|url = http://www.ibtimes.co.uk/dr-gideon-greenspan-blockchain-design-academic-work-shouldnt-just-be-decided-by-banks-1520754|website = International Business Times UK|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+IBTimesUK/posts|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> appending a block is dependent on one authorised validator, or a quorum of the validator set, signing that block with their private keys.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Explainer {{!}} Permissioned Blockchains|url = https://docs.erisindustries.com/explainers/permissioned_blockchains/|website = Eris Industries Documentation|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref>

==Peer-to-peer network architecture==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a blockchain network has a complete or partial copy of the block chain, allowing a blockchain to be run as a [[peer-to-peer]] application.. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require where the [[Master/slave (technology)|master/slave]] model dictates the relationship between the user and the provider of the application.{{r|"primer"}} 

With cryptocurrencies, transactions take the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' and are broadcast to this network using protocol implementations, often with several implementations existing concurrently for one blockchain (e.g. BTCD, BitcoinXT, BitcoinJ). Network nodes can then validate these transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and  [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending;{{R|t3}} other blockchains use them to timestamp other types of data, e.g. state transitions of smart contracts.<ref>{{Cite web|title = ethereum/wiki|url = https://github.com/ethereum/wiki/wiki/White-Paper|website = GitHub|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> Blockchains use various timestamping schemes, including [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency{{r|kopstein}} or otherwise writing an unauthorised transaction to the blockchain in question.

== Messages ==
The bitcoin blockchain can perform a number of functions other than value transfer, most of which any other blockchain design can replicate to a certain extent. For example, a blockchain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various third party application services can store messages directly in a blockchain, so anyone who has a copy of that blockchain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the Bitcoin blockchain, in particular, criticizing the practice as "bloat".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications such as Factom are designed to store a hash value in the blockchain, demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>
[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].
</ref>

This "extra information" in the blockchain can be used to implement "colored coins" and other [[smart contract]]s. Such functionality is seen in the Counterparty and OpenAssets protocols on top of Bitcoin, and numerous new chain designs which have been built from scratch. 

==Development trends==

=== Cryptocurrencies ===
With cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin, the meaning of the data (Bitcoins) is ''intrinsic'' to the blockchain; that is to say, whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}} With non-cryptocurrency types, however, blockchains act simply as a communications system and a shared record between the various participants of the network.

Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>
Building upon the underlying technology of the blockchain, a variety of applications have subsequently been developed, adapting the bitcoin protocol to fulfill specific purposes: e.g. Namecoin for distributed domain name management, Swarm and Koinify for decentralized crowd-funding, Synereo for synchronous and asynchronous communication, LaZooz for decentralized and real-time ride sharing. More generally, [[Ethereum]] has recently been launched, as a general purpose blockchain for the deployment of decentralized applications. Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate the figure of the middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}

=== Permissioned blockchains ===
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain system without a native token which requires an access control layer to run and secure - also known as "permissioned" blockchains - should continue to be called a blockchain,{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}  with Bitcoin advocates generally supporting a narrower, Bitcoin-specific definition, and "blockchain 2.0" platforms generally supporting a wider definition which hinges on whether a given "blockchain" design uses both [[Timestamp|timestamping]] and a [[merkle tree]]. Most of the well-publicised newer blockchain implementations for enterprise use both.<ref>{{Cite web|title = IBM Creates Open-Source Blockchain With Linux and Big Banks - CoinDesk|url = http://www.coindesk.com/ibm-launches-open-source-blockchain-project-backed-by-linux-and-big-banks/|website = CoinDesk|publisher = https://plus.google.com/+Coindesk/|accessdate = 2015-12-17|language = en-US}}</ref>   

Where these databases fall within existing database taxonomies, however, remains an open question. Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[Multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Implementations ==
{{Prose|section|date=November 2015}}

=== Original bitcoin protocol blockchain ===
The bitcoin network uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

==== Sidechain implementations ====
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from [[Blockstream]]
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform from ChromaWay
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - SEC approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

=== Newer chain designs ===
Networks running software based on the bitcoin protocol but using alternative cryptocurrency tokens (known as altcoins){{R|t13}} or implementations which are simply database designs in the classical sense exist. These newer designs generally provide additional features or functionality which earlier cryptocurrency designs such as bitcoin's did not. Notable designs include: 

'''Altcoins'''

* [[Namecoin]] – Cryptocurrency merge-mined{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Cryptocurrency metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Cryptocurrency supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses on its blockchain, with a 15 second block time
"'''Blockchain 2.0'''" '''platforms'''
