{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a permissionless [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of transactional data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and most widely known application of the block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} which would become the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

== Name ==
The block chain is is primarily [[Tamper-evident technology|tamper resistant]] through [[Trusted timestamping|timestamping]] the [[Hash function|hash]] of batches of recent valid transactions into "blocks", [[Proof-of-work system|proving]] that the data must have existed at the time. Each block includes the prior timestamp, forming a chain of blocks, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name. Each block chain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as data store nodes extending this validation to the network as a whole.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became [[Journaling file system|journaled]] as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a best-effort basis. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In most cryptocurrency applications,{{cn|date=December 2015}} a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional ledger records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that bitcoins can be said to exist in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Token-less blockchain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}. Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Data storage ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin block chain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the block chain, so anyone who has the block chain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the [[bitcoin network|bitcoin block chain]], criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This extra information in the block chain can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

== Bitcoin sidechain implementations ==
The [[bitcoin network]] uses the original [[Bitcoin#Block chain|bitcoin block chain]] based on [[proof of work]].

Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol, and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with the blockchain. Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain from [[Blockstream]]
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]] approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

== Alternative chain designs ==
Networks running software based on the bitcoin protocol but using alternative cryptocurrency tokens (known as altcoins){{R|t13}} or implementations which are simply database designs in the classical sense exist. These newer designs generally provide additional features or functionality which earlier cryptocurrency designs such as bitcoin's did not. Developments from the first implementation, bitcoin, have built additional features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}
Notable designs include: 

* [[Namecoin]] – Cryptocurrency merge-mined{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Cryptocurrency metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Cryptocurrency supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses on its blockchain, with a 15 second block time
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - Regulated "[[cryptocash]]" blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* Swarm and Koinify - decentralized [[crowdfunding]]
* Synereo - [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]
* LaZooz - decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]

== See also ==
* [[Bitcoin network]]
* [[Colored coins]]

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a permissionless [[distributed database]] network based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and most widely known application of the block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

== Name ==
The block chain is primarily [[Tamper-evident technology|tamper resistant]] through [[Trusted timestamping|timestamping]] the [[Hash function|hash]] of batches of recent valid transactions into "blocks", [[Proof-of-work system|proving]] that the data must have existed at the time. Each block includes the prior timestamp, forming a chain of blocks, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name. Each block chain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as [[Node (networking)|data store nodes]] extending this validation to the network as a whole.{{R|bc1}}  

==Basic principles==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became [[Journaling file system|journaled]] as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist [[Fiat money|apart from it]], the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Token-less block chain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}. Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Data storage ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin block chain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the block chain, so anyone who has the block chain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the [[bitcoin network|bitcoin block chain]], criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This extra information in the block chain can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

== Sidechain Technology ==
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol and are isolated from the blockchain allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the blockchain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with network(s) their technology is designed to support (the blockchain, altchains, multiple networks, etc...). Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain being developed by [[Blockstream]] 
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]] approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

== Alternative designs ==
Alternative or alternate blockchains (commonly known as altchains) are networks based on the technology that underpins bitcoin and are based on its technology in concept and/or its software code base. These designs generally provide additional or different features or functionality not present in the original blockchain design. Developments from the first implementation have built additional or alternative features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}
Notable designs include: 

* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency [[Merged mining|merge-mined]]{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency based token incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15 second block time. It uses Ether as its token.
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - Regulated "cryptocash" blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* Swarm and Koinify - decentralized [[crowdfunding]]. {{citation needed|date=January 2015|[[WP:NOTABILITY]]}}
* [[Synereo]] - [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]  {{citation needed|date=January 2015|[[WP:NOTABILITY]]}}
* LaZooz - decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]  {{citation needed|date=January 2015|[[WP:NOTABILITY]]}}

==  Blockchains and right management in the music industry ==<ref name=bc3>{{cite web|title=Blockchain will become the operating system for music?|url=https://medium.com/@GetRevelator/blockchain-322f4366b0ce#.c5zlgfmf1}}</ref>
The [[Music_industry|music industry]] suffers from a lack of information that penalize right holders such as artists, composers, song writers ...As we can not systematically identify the exhaustive lists of right holders of a song, some payees are being left out. {{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a permissionless [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and most widely known application of the block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

== Name ==
The block chain is primarily [[Tamper-evident technology|tamper resistant]] through [[Trusted timestamping|timestamping]] the [[Hash function|hash]] of batches of recent valid transactions into "blocks", [[Proof-of-work system|proving]] that the data must have existed at the time. Each block includes the prior timestamp, forming a chain of blocks, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name. Each block chain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as [[Node (networking)|data store nodes]] extending this validation to the network as a whole.{{R|bc1}}  

==Basic principles==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became [[Journaling file system|journaled]] as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist [[Fiat money|apart from it]], the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Token-less block chain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native token can still be considered a blockchain{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}. Some have argued that token-free block chains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain. 

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Data storage ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin block chain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the block chain, so anyone who has the block chain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the [[bitcoin network|bitcoin block chain]], criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This extra information in the block chain can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

== Sidechain Technology ==
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol and are isolated from the main block chain network, allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the block chain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with network(s) their technology is designed to support (the block chain, altchains, multiple networks, etc...). Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain being developed by [[Blockstream]] 
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]] approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

== Alternative blockchains ==
Alternative or alternate blockchains (commonly known as altchains) are based on the technology that underpins bitcoin in concept and/or its software code base. These designs generally provide different or alternate functionality or additional utility not present in the initial blockchain design. Altchains can be operated in private or public environments providing a wide variety of solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered as such. Development of these designs have produced features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}
Notable designs include: 

* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency [[Merged mining|merge-mined]]{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency based token incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15 second block time. It uses Ether as its token.
* [[Billon_standard|Billon]] - Regulated "cryptocash" blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* Swarm and Koinify - decentralized [[crowdfunding]]. {{citation needed|date=January 2016|[[WP:NOTABILITY]]}}
* [[Synereo]] - [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]  {{citation needed|date=January 2016|[[WP:NOTABILITY]]}}
* LaZooz - decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]  {{citation needed|date=January 2016|[[WP:NOTABILITY]]}}
* [[Nxt]] – Cryptocurrency financial platform that uses [[proof of stake]] to reach consensus for transactions. It has an integrated Asset Exchange, messaging system and marketplace.

The [[Music_industry|music industry]] suffers from a lack of information that penalizes right holders such as artists, composers, song writers ...

As we can not systematically identify the exhaustive lists of right holders of a song, some payees are being left out. Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol and are isolated from the main block chain network, allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the block chain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with network(s) their technology is designed to support (the block chain, altchains, multiple networks, etc...). Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain being developed by [[Blockstream]] 
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]] approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

== Alternative blockchains ==
Alternative or alternate blockchains (commonly known as altchains) are based on the technology that underpins bitcoin in concept and/or its software code base. These designs generally provide different or alternate functionality or additional utility not present in the initial blockchain design. Altchains can be operated in private or public environments providing a wide variety of solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered as such. Development of these designs have produced features for performance, anonymity, storage and [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, uses of blockchain technology are progressively extending to many new sectors of activities, including the creation of decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}}
Notable designs include:

* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency [[Merged mining|merge-mined]]{{clarify|date=December 2015|reason=the term is unexplained}} with bitcoin possessing the ability to store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency based token incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15 second block time. It uses Ether as its token.
* [[Billon standard|Billon]] - Regulated "cryptocash" blockchain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* Swarm and Koinify - decentralized [[crowdfunding]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Ayral|first1=Sandrine|title=Bitcoin 2.0 Crowdfunding Is Real Crowdfunding|url=http://techcrunch.com/2014/10/17/bitcoin-2-0-crowdfunding-is-real-crowdfunding/|website=Techcrunch|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* Synereo - [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Miller|first1=Carl|title=In 2015 social media companies strengthened their rule. In 2016 they will face rebellion|url=http://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/social-media/12061333/In-2015-social-media-companies-strengthened-their-rule.-In-2016-they-will-face-rebellion.html|website=The Telegraph|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* LaZooz - decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Coppola, Benmeleh|first1=Gabrielle, Yaacov|title=This Israeli Ride-Sharing App Is the Utopian, Hippie Uber|url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-09-16/this-israeli-ride-sharing-app-is-the-utopian-hippie-uber|website=Bloomberg|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* [[Nxt]] – Cryptocurrency financial platform that uses [[proof of stake]] to reach consensus for transactions. It has an integrated Asset Exchange, messaging system and marketplace.

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain'''  is a permissionless [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by operators of the data store's [[Node (networking)|nodes]]. The initial and most widely known application of the block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]]{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

== Name ==
The block chain is primarily [[Tamper-evident technology|tamper resistant]] through [[Trusted timestamping|timestamping]] the [[Hash function|hash]] of batches of recent valid transactions into "blocks", [[Proof-of-work system|proving]] that the data must have existed at the time. Each block includes the prior timestamp, forming a chain of blocks, with each additional timestamp reinforcing the ones before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name. Each block chain record is enforced [[cryptography|cryptographically]] and hosted on machines working as [[Node (networking)|data store nodes]] extending this validation to the network as a whole.{{R|bc1}}

==Basic principles==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for a significant number of nodes to converge on a single [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the most up-to-date version of a large data set such as a ledger, even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor connectivity with one another, and have operators who may be dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any node that is well-connected to other nodes to determine, with a reasonable level of certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the confirmed data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a certain period of confirmation time, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place, and become final (i.e. that there were no conflicting transactions confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would make the transaction invalid, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter any transaction history.
* An automated form of resolution that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. Transactions are the actual data to be stored in the block chain, and blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions became [[Journaling file system|journaled]] as a part of the block chain database. Transactions are created by participants using the system in the normal course of business and blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime someone sends cryptocurrency to another.

Users of the system create transactions which are loosely passed around from node to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The definition of what constitutes a valid transaction is based on the system implementing the block chain. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction is one that is properly digitally signed, spends one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs does not exceed the sum of inputs.

Meanwhile, miners attempt to create blocks that confirm and incorporate those transactions into the blockchain. In a cryptocurrency system such as bitcoin, miners are incentivized to create blocks in order to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Decentralisation==
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency has a complete or partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database that other systems, such as PayPal, require.{{r|"primer"}} Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist [[Fiat money|apart from it]], the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy of the ledger, and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions added to the block chain. This avoids anyone easily [[double-spending]] the currency.{{r|kopstein}}

==Cryptocurrency-less block chain debate ==
There is an ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} as to whether a blockchain-like system without a native cryptocurrency can still be considered a blockchain.{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}} Some have argued that cryptocurrency-less block chains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from modifying a single row in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain.

Others have interpreted blockchains' function in a different way, arguing that blockchains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Data storage ==
Cryptocurrencies use block chains to timestamp transactions to prevent double-spending.

The bitcoin block chain can also be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages, not just transaction information. Various 3rd party application services store messages directly in the block chain, so anyone who has the block chain can read the message.<ref>
Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>
Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>
[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others have discouraged embedding large messages in the [[bitcoin network|bitcoin block chain]], criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>
Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>
[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>
Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain,
demonstrating data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing actual data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This extra information in the block chain can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

Sidechains{{R|t5}} are private or public networks that may or may not be based on the bitcoin protocol and are isolated from the main block chain network, allowing workflow and functionality to exist in confinement until confirmation on the block chain is required or desired, at which point bidirectional transferability is supported. These systems work in conjunction with network(s) their technology is designed to support (the block chain, altchains, multiple networks, etc...). Examples are

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain being developed by Blockstream
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]] approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]

Alternative or alternate blockchains (commonly known as altchains) are based on the technology that underpins bitcoin in concept and/or its software code base. Notable designs include:

* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol to bitcoin with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency based token incorporating the [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15 second block time. It uses Ether as its token.

In 2016 they will face rebellion|url=http://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/social-media/12061333/In-2015-social-media-companies-strengthened-their-rule.-In-2016-they-will-face-rebellion.html|website=The Telegraph|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a permissionless [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol{{R|t3}} that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by its operators. The initial and most widely known application of block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for [[bitcoin]],{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

== Etymology ==
The block chain consists of ''blocks'' that hold [[Trusted timestamping|timestamped]] batches of recent valid transactions. Each block includes the [[Hash function|hash]] of the prior block, linking the blocks together. The linked blocks form a ''chain'', with each additional block reinforcing those before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.{{R|bc1}}

== Model ==
A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. 

=== Transactions ===
Transactions are the content to be stored in the block chain.Transactions are created by participants using the system. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created anytime a bitcoin owner sends cryptocurrency to another.

System users create transactions that are passed from node to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

=== Decentralisation ===
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency system has at least a partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database.{{r|"primer"}} Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions. This prevents users from [[double-spending]] their coins.{{r|kopstein}}

Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates ==
An ongoing{{R|t16}} debate{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}} disputes whether a block chain-like system without a native cryptocurrency can still be considered a block chain.{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Some have argued that cryptocurrency-less block chains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from concurrently modifying a single object in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain.

Others have interpreted block chains in a different way, arguing that block chains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Applications ==
===  Sidechains ===
Sidechains{{R|t5}} are networks based on the bitcoin protocol that are isolated from the block chain, allowing activity to exist in isolation until confirmation on the block chain, at which point transferability becomes bidirectional. Examples:

* Liquid{{R|t6}} - Exchange sidechain developed by Blockstream
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]] approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]
===  Alternative block chains ===
Alternate block chains (altchains) are based on bitcoin technology in concept and/or code. These designs generally add functionalty to the block chain design. Altchains can provide solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered to be such. Altchains target performance, anonymity, storage and applications such as [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, block chain technology is extending to activities including decentralized applications and collaborative organisations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}} Notable designs include:

* [[Billon standard|Billon]] – Regulated "cryptocash" block chain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15-second block time. Uses Ether as its token.
* [[Hyperledger]] – aims to create an open-standard, public, decentralised public ledger based on blockchain technology.
* LaZooz – decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Coppola, Benmeleh|first1=Gabrielle, Yaacov|title=This Israeli Ride-Sharing App Is the Utopian, Hippie Uber|url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-09-16/this-israeli-ride-sharing-app-is-the-utopian-hippie-uber|website=Bloomberg|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency that can store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Nxt]] – Cryptocurrency financial platform that uses proof of stake to reach consensus for transactions. It has an integrated Asset Exchange, messaging system and marketplace.
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency-based token incorporating [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* Swarm and Koinify – decentralized [[crowdfunding]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Ayral|first1=Sandrine|title=Bitcoin 2.0 Crowdfunding Is Real Crowdfunding|url=http://techcrunch.com/2014/10/17/bitcoin-2-0-crowdfunding-is-real-crowdfunding/|website=Techcrunch|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* Synereo – [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Miller|first1=Carl|title=In 2015 social media companies strengthened their rule. In 2016 they will face rebellion|url=http://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/social-media/12061333/In-2015-social-media-companies-strengthened-their-rule.-In-2016-they-will-face-rebellion.html|website=The Telegraph|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>

===  Rights management ===
The [[music industry]] lacks complete information about the rights holders for musical works. These include performers, composers and songwriters.<ref name="music bc">{{cite web|title=Blockchain will become the operating system for music?|url=https://medium.com/@GetRevelator/blockchain-322f4366b0ce#.c5zlgfmf1}}</ref> {{Better source|reason=self-published|date=January 2016}} Block chain technology can be used to 'provide the secure issuance and distribution of digital assets, including listing and registration of musical works for its clients and helping collecting societies provide more transparency and efficiency to all market participants'.<ref name="bc3">{{cite web|title=Blockchain will fix music data |url=http://www.billboard.com/biz/articles/6664105/blockchain-platform-colu-partners-with-revelator-in-push-to-fix-musics-data}}</ref>

=== Trusted timestamp ===
The bitcoin block chain can be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages. Third party application services store messages directly in the block chain, allowing anyone who has the block chain to read the message.<ref>Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others discouraged embedding large messages in the bitcoin block chain, criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain, recording data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This information can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a permissionless [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by its operators.{{R|t3}} The initial and most widely known application of block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for bitcoin,{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

== Etymology ==
The block chain consists of ''blocks'' that hold [[Trusted timestamping|timestamped]] batches of recent valid transactions. Each block includes the [[Hash function|hash]] of the prior block, linking the blocks together. The linked blocks form a ''chain'', with each additional block reinforcing those before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.{{R|bc1}} The original definition was written by Satoshi Nakamoto and found in the original source code of bitcoin.<ref name="original-bitcoin main.h">{{cite web|last1=Nakamoto|first1=Satoshi|title=original-bitcoin main.h|url=https://github.com/trottier/original-bitcoin/blob/master/src/main.h#L795-L803|publisher=Satoshi Nakamoto|accessdate=2009}}</ref>

== Model ==
A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. 

=== Transactions ===
Transactions are the content to be stored in the block chain. Transactions are created by participants using the system. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created any time a bitcoin owner sends cryptocurrency to another.

System users create transactions that are passed from [[Node (networking)|node]]  to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

=== Decentralisation ===
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency system has at least a partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database.{{r|"primer"}} Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions. This prevents users from [[double-spending]] their coins.{{r|kopstein}}

Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates ==
An ongoing debate disputes whether a block chain-like system without a native cryptocurrency can still be considered a block chain.{{R|t16}}{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Some have argued that cryptocurrency-less block chains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from concurrently modifying a single object in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain.

Others have interpreted block chains in a different way, arguing that block chains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

<ref name=t3>{{cite web | url=https://bitcoin.org/bitcoin.pdf | title=Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System | publisher=Bitcoin.org | year=2008 | accessdate=31 October 2008 | author=Satoshi Nakamoto }}</ref>
