{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a permissionless [[distributed database]] based on the [[bitcoin]] protocol that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by its operators.{{R|t3}} The initial and most widely known application of block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for bitcoin,{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

Other distinguishing characteristics of blockchains include various means to achieve [[Byzantine fault tolerance|consensus across their networks]], such as [[Proof-of-work system|proof of work]], [[Proof-of-stake|proof of stake]],<ref>{{Cite web|title = What Proof of Stake Is And Why It Matters|url = https://bitcoinmagazine.com/articles/what-proof-of-stake-is-and-why-it-matters-1377531463|website = Bitcoin Magazine|accessdate = 2015-12-17}}</ref> as well as controlling write permissions to the blockchain through the use of cryptographic [[Digital Signature Algorithm|digital signatures]].<ref>{{Cite web|title = How do digital signatures in Bitcoin work?|url = https://www.cryptocompare.com/wallets/guides/how-do-digital-signatures-in-bitcoin-work/|website = CryptoCompare|publisher = CryptoCompare|accessdate = 17 December 2015|date=16 September 2015}}</ref>

== Etymology ==
The block chain consists of ''blocks'' that hold [[Trusted timestamping|timestamped]] batches of recent valid transactions. Each block includes the [[Hash function|hash]] of the prior block, linking the blocks together. The linked blocks form a ''chain'', with each additional block reinforcing those before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.{{R|bc1}} The original definition was written by Satoshi Nakamoto and found in the original source code of bitcoin.<ref name="original-bitcoin main.h">{{cite web|last1=Nakamoto|first1=Satoshi|title=original-bitcoin main.h|url=https://github.com/trottier/original-bitcoin/blob/master/src/main.h#L795-L803|publisher=Satoshi Nakamoto|accessdate=2009}}</ref>

== Model ==
A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. 

=== Transactions ===
Transactions are the content to be stored in the block chain. Transactions are created by participants using the system. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created any time a bitcoin owner sends cryptocurrency to another.

System users create transactions that are passed from [[Node (networking)|node]]  to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

=== Decentralisation ===
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency system has at least a partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database.{{r|"primer"}} Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions. This prevents users from [[double-spending]] their coins.{{r|kopstein}}

Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates on classification==

An ongoing debate disputes whether a block chain-like system without a native cryptocurrency should still be considered a block chain.{{R|t16}}{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Proponents of permissioned or private chains argue that the term "blockchain" is rightly applied to any data structure which batches data into blocks which are timestamped and that these blockchains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from concurrently modifying a single object in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a blockchain.

Others have interpreted block chains in a different way, arguing that block chains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Newer uses of blockchains following Bitcoin ==

Newer blockchain designs are based on the same principles of Bitcoin conceptually, but differ in the code and/or cryptographic primitives used to implement them, and the applications they are designed to solve.

These designs generally add functionality to the block chain design above and beyond the simple linked list/UTXO scripting which Bitcoin supports; newer blockchains can provide solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered to be such. They target performance, anonymity, storage and applications such as [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> 

Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, blockchain technology is extending to activities including decentralized applications and collaborative organizations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}} 

=== Trusted timestamp ===
The bitcoin block chain can be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages. Third party application services store messages directly in the block chain, allowing anyone who has the block chain to read the message.<ref>Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin Core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others discouraged embedding large messages in the bitcoin block chain, criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain, recording data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This information can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

=== Other uses ===
[[Cooperative storage cloud]] solutions sometimes employ block chain technology to regulate exchange of data or actual payments for storage space.

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a [[distributed database]] that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by its operators.{{R|t3}} The client programs running any one such distributed database form a '''blockchain network'''. 

The initial and most widely known application of blockchain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for the software application Bitcoin,{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for new implementations which may or may not be similar in function or design to the Bitcoin protocol.{{R|t13}}

The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates on classification==

An ongoing debate disputes whether a private system with verifiers tasked and authorized (permissioned) by a central authority, should still be considered a block chain.{{R|t16}}{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Proponents of permissioned or private chains argue that the term "blockchain" is rightly applied to any data structure which batches data into blocks which are timestamped. Depending on the type of blockchain data structure involved, proponents classify these blockchains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases (in the case of [[Bitcoin]]-derived UTXO-based systems){{R|t4}} or [[finite-state machine]] automata (in the case of [[Ethereum]]-derived account based systems).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|title=Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|website=Engineering DAPPs|access-date=2016-03-09}}</ref>

Systems such as these which utilise permissions layers are commonly described as "blockchains" and increasingly seen in commercial use among financial institutions<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.coindesk.com/r3-consortium-banks-blockchain-solutions/|title=40 Banks Trial Commercial Paper Trading in Latest R3 Blockchain Test - CoinDesk|website=CoinDesk|language=en-US|access-date=2016-03-09}}</ref>, systems integrators<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.coindesk.com/pwc-fintech-director-disruptive-blockchain-could-create-winners-and-losers/|title=PwC Director: Blockchain Impact Could Create Winners and Losers - CoinDesk|website=CoinDesk|language=en-US|access-date=2016-03-09}}</ref> and technology providers<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.coindesk.com/microsoft-blockchain-azure-marley-gray/|title=Why Microsoft Wants 'Every Blockchain' on its Azure Platform - CoinDesk|website=CoinDesk|language=en-US|access-date=2016-03-09}}</ref> alike. 

Opponents say that the permissioned systems look like traditional corporate databases, not supporting decentralized verification of the data.{{R|t11}}{{R|t16}}

Newer designs are based on bitcoin conceptually, but differ in the code and/or cryptographic primitives used to implement them, and the applications they are designed to solve. These designs add functionality above and beyond the functionality bitcoin block chain supports; they can provide solutions including other digital currencies, target performance, anonymity, storage and applications such as [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref>

=== Transactions ===
Transactions are the content to be stored in the block chain. Transactions are created by participants using the system. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created any time a bitcoin owner sends cryptocurrency to another.

System users create transactions that are passed from [[Node (networking)|node]]  to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

=== Decentralisation ===
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency system has at least a partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database.{{r|"primer"}} Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions. This prevents users from [[double-spending]] their coins.{{r|kopstein}}

Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates ==
An ongoing debate disputes whether a block chain-like system without a native cryptocurrency can still be considered a block chain.{{R|t16}}{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Some have argued that cryptocurrency-less block chains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from concurrently modifying a single object in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a block chain.

Others have interpreted block chains in a different way, arguing that block chains are more akin to [[Finite-state machine|finite state machine]] automata "where the state of the system is updated sequentially, via atomic transitions (transactions) that are replicated across every machine, in order."<ref>{{Cite web|title = Secrets of Consistent Hashchains I: Eventual Consistency|url = https://eng.erisindustries.com/blockchains/2014/12/22/secrets-of-consistent-hashchains-I/|website = Engineering DAPPs|accessdate = 2015-11-02}}</ref>

== Applications ==
===  Sidechains ===
Sidechains are networks based on the bitcoin protocol that are isolated from the block chain, allowing activity to exist in isolation until confirmation on the block chain, at which point transferability becomes bidirectional.{{R|t5}} Examples:

* Liquid - Exchange sidechain developed by Blockstream{{R|t6}}
* ChromaWay - Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS - Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) - [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]]-approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]
* [https://docs.openchain.org Openchain] – NoConsensus NoP2P modular distributed ledger technology for asset management, bidirectional pegging, and chain anchoring (or "sidechaining").
===  Alternative block chains ===
Alternate block chains (altchains) are based on bitcoin technology in concept and/or code. These designs generally add functionality to the block chain design. Altchains can provide solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered to be such. Altchains target performance, anonymity, storage and applications such as [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, block chain technology is extending to activities including decentralized applications and collaborative organizations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}} Notable designs include:

* [[Billon standard|Billon]] – Regulated "cryptocash" block chain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15-second block time. Uses Ether as its token.
* LaZooz – decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Coppola, Benmeleh|first1=Gabrielle, Yaacov|title=This Israeli Ride-Sharing App Is the Utopian, Hippie Uber|url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-09-16/this-israeli-ride-sharing-app-is-the-utopian-hippie-uber|website=Bloomberg|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency that can store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Nxt]] – Cryptocurrency financial platform that uses proof of stake to reach consensus for transactions. It has an integrated Asset Exchange, messaging system and marketplace.
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency-based token incorporating [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* Swarm and Koinify – decentralized [[crowdfunding]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Ayral|first1=Sandrine|title=Bitcoin 2.0 Crowdfunding Is Real Crowdfunding|url=http://techcrunch.com/2014/10/17/bitcoin-2-0-crowdfunding-is-real-crowdfunding/|website=Techcrunch|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* Synereo – [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Miller|first1=Carl|title=In 2015 social media companies strengthened their rule. In 2016 they will face rebellion|url=http://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/social-media/12061333/In-2015-social-media-companies-strengthened-their-rule.-In-2016-they-will-face-rebellion.html|website=The Telegraph|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>

=== Trusted timestamp ===
The bitcoin block chain can be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages. Third party application services store messages directly in the block chain, allowing anyone who has the block chain to read the message.<ref>Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin Core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others discouraged embedding large messages in the bitcoin block chain, criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain, recording data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This information can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

=== Other uses ===
[[Cooperative storage cloud]] solutions sometimes employ block chain technology to regulate exchange of data or actual payments for storage space.

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a [[distributed database]], based on the [[bitcoin protocol]], that maintains a continuously growing list of data records hardened against tampering and revision, even by its operators.{{R|t3}} The initial and most widely known application of block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for bitcoin,{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for similar implementations often known as altchains.{{R|t13}}

== Etymology ==
The block chain consists of ''blocks'' that hold [[Trusted timestamping|timestamped]] batches of recent valid transactions. Each block includes the [[Hash function|hash]] of the prior block, linking the blocks together. The linked blocks form a ''chain'', with each additional block reinforcing those before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.{{R|bc1}} The original definition was written by [[Satoshi Nakamoto]] and found in the original source code of bitcoin.<ref name="original-bitcoin main.h">{{cite web|last1=Nakamoto|first1=Satoshi|title=original-bitcoin main.h|url=https://github.com/trottier/original-bitcoin/blob/master/src/main.h#L795-L803|publisher=Satoshi Nakamoto|accessdate=2009}}</ref>

== Model ==
A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. 

=== Transactions ===
Transactions are the content to be stored in the block chain. Transactions are created by participants using the system. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created any time a bitcoin owner sends cryptocurrency to another.

System users create transactions that are passed from [[Node (networking)|node]]  to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

=== Decentralisation ===
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency system has at least a partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database.{{r|"primer"}} Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions. This prevents users from [[double-spending]] their coins.{{r|kopstein}}

Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates ==
An ongoing debate disputes whether a private system with verifiers tasked and authorized (permissioned) by a central authority, should still be considered a block chain.{{R|t16}}{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Proponents of permissioned or private chains argue that the term "blockchain" is rightly applied to any data structure which batches data into blocks which are timestamped and that these blockchains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from concurrently modifying a single object in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a blockchain.

The opponents say that the permissioned systems look like traditional corporate databases, not supporting decentralized verification of the data, and that such systems are not hardened against tampering and revision by its operators.{{R|t11}}{{R|t16}}

===  Sidechains ===
Sidechains are networks based on the bitcoin protocol that are isolated from the block chain, allowing activity to exist in isolation until confirmation on the block chain, at which point transferability becomes bidirectional.{{R|t5}} Examples:

<ref name=t3>{{cite web | url=https://bitcoin.org/bitcoin.pdf | title=Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System | publisher=Bitcoin.org | year=2008 | accessdate=31 October 2008 | author=Satoshi Nakamoto }}</ref>

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a [[distributed database]], introduced in [[Bitcoin]], that maintains a continuously-growing list of data records that each refer to previous items on this list and is thus hardened against tampering and revision.{{R|t3}}

The initial and most widely known application of block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for bitcoin,{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for other [[cryptocurrencies]]{{R|t13}} and [[distributed database]] designs.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.coindesk.com/r3-consortium-banks-blockchain-solutions/|title=40 Banks Trial Commercial Paper Trading in Latest R3 Blockchain Test - CoinDesk|website=CoinDesk|language=en-US|access-date=2016-03-21}}</ref>

== Etymology ==
The block chain consists of ''blocks'' that hold [[Trusted timestamping|timestamped]] batches of recent valid transactions. Each block includes the [[Hash function|hash]] of the prior block, linking the blocks together. The linked blocks form a ''chain'', with each additional block reinforcing those before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.{{R|bc1}} The original definition was written by [[Satoshi Nakamoto]] and found in the original source code of bitcoin.<ref name="original-bitcoin main.h">{{cite web|last1=Nakamoto|first1=Satoshi|title=original-bitcoin main.h|url=https://github.com/trottier/original-bitcoin/blob/master/src/main.h#L795-L803|publisher=Satoshi Nakamoto|accessdate=2009}}</ref>

== Model ==
A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. 

=== Transactions ===
Transactions are the content to be stored in the block chain. Transactions are created by participants using the system. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created any time a bitcoin owner sends cryptocurrency to another.

System users create transactions that are passed from [[Node (networking)|node]]  to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

=== Decentralisation ===
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency system has at least a partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database.{{r|"primer"}} Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions. This prevents users from [[double-spending]] their coins.{{r|kopstein}}

Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates ==
An ongoing debate disputes whether a private system with verifiers tasked and authorized (permissioned) by a central authority, should still be considered a block chain.{{R|t16}}{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Proponents of permissioned or private chains argue that the term "blockchain" is rightly applied to any data structure which batches data into blocks which are timestamped and that these blockchains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from concurrently modifying a single object in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a blockchain.

The opponents say that the permissioned systems look like traditional corporate databases, not supporting decentralized verification of the data, and that such systems are not hardened against tampering and revision by its operators.{{R|t11}}{{R|t16}}

== Applications ==
===  Sidechains ===
Sidechains are networks based on the bitcoin protocol that are isolated from the block chain, allowing activity to exist in isolation until confirmation on the block chain, at which point transferability becomes bidirectional.{{R|t5}} Examples:

* Liquid – Exchange sidechain developed by Blockstream{{R|t6}}
* ChromaWay – Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS – Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) – [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]]-approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]
* [https://docs.openchain.org Openchain] – NoConsensus NoP2P modular distributed ledger technology for asset management, bidirectional pegging, and chain anchoring (or "sidechaining").

===  Alternative block chains ===
Alternate block chains (altchains) are based on bitcoin technology in concept and/or code.<ref>{{cite web|title=Blockchain|url=http://www.investopedia.com/terms/b/blockchain.asp|website=Investopedia|accessdate=19 March 2016|quote=Based on the Bitcoin protocol, the blockchain database is shared by all nodes participating in a system.}}</ref> These designs generally add functionality to the block chain design. Altchains can provide solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered to be such. Altchains target performance, anonymity, storage and applications such as [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, block chain technology is extending to activities including decentralized applications and collaborative organizations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}} Notable designs include:

* [[Billon standard|Billon]] – Regulated "cryptocash" block chain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15-second block time. Uses Ether as its token.
* LaZooz – decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Coppola, Benmeleh|first1=Gabrielle, Yaacov|title=This Israeli Ride-Sharing App Is the Utopian, Hippie Uber|url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-09-16/this-israeli-ride-sharing-app-is-the-utopian-hippie-uber|website=Bloomberg|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency that can store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Nxt]] – Cryptocurrency financial platform that uses proof of stake to reach consensus for transactions. It has an integrated Asset Exchange, messaging system and marketplace.
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency-based token incorporating [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* Swarm and Koinify – decentralized [[crowdfunding]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Ayral|first1=Sandrine|title=Bitcoin 2.0 Crowdfunding Is Real Crowdfunding|url=http://techcrunch.com/2014/10/17/bitcoin-2-0-crowdfunding-is-real-crowdfunding/|website=Techcrunch|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* Synereo – [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Miller|first1=Carl|title=In 2015 social media companies strengthened their rule. In 2016 they will face rebellion|url=http://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/social-media/12061333/In-2015-social-media-companies-strengthened-their-rule.-In-2016-they-will-face-rebellion.html|website=The Telegraph|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>

=== Trusted timestamp ===
The bitcoin block chain can be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages. Third party application services store messages directly in the block chain, allowing anyone who has the block chain to read the message.<ref>Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin Core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others discouraged embedding large messages in the bitcoin block chain, criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain, recording data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This information can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

=== Other uses ===
[[Cooperative storage cloud]] solutions sometimes employ block chain technology to regulate exchange of data or actual payments for storage space.

{{Cryptocurrencies}}

[[Category:Cryptocurrencies]]
[[Category:Bitcoin]]
[[Category:Blockchains]]

A '''block chain''' or '''blockchain''' is a [[distributed database]], introduced in [[Bitcoin]], that maintains a continuously-growing list of data records that each refer to previous items on this list and is thus hardened against tampering and revision.{{R|t3}}

The initial and most widely known application of block chain technology is the public [[ledger]] of [[Transaction processing|transactions]] for bitcoin,{{R|t12}} which has been the inspiration for other [[cryptocurrencies]]{{R|t13}} and [[distributed database]] designs.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.coindesk.com/r3-consortium-banks-blockchain-solutions/|title=40 Banks Trial Commercial Paper Trading in Latest R3 Blockchain Test - CoinDesk|website=CoinDesk|language=en-US|access-date=2016-03-21}}</ref>

== Etymology ==
The block chain consists of ''blocks'' that hold [[Trusted timestamping|timestamped]] batches of recent valid transactions. Each block includes the [[Hash function|hash]] of the prior block, linking the blocks together. The linked blocks form a ''chain'', with each additional block reinforcing those before it,{{R|t3}} thus giving the database type its name.{{R|bc1}} The original definition was written by [[Satoshi Nakamoto]] and found in the original source code of bitcoin.<ref name="original-bitcoin main.h">{{cite web|last1=Nakamoto|first1=Satoshi|title=original-bitcoin main.h|url=https://github.com/trottier/original-bitcoin/blob/master/src/main.h#L795-L803|publisher=Satoshi Nakamoto|accessdate=2009}}</ref>

== Model ==
A block chain implementation consists of two kinds of records: transactions and blocks. 

=== Transactions ===
Transactions are the content to be stored in the block chain. Transactions are created by participants using the system. In the case of cryptocurrencies, a transaction is created any time a cryptocurrency owner sends cryptocurrency to someone.

System users create transactions that are passed from [[Node (networking)|node]]  to node on a [[Best-effort delivery|best-effort basis]]. The system implementing the block chain defines a valid transaction. In cryptocurrency applications, a valid transaction must be digitally signed, spend one or more unspent outputs of previous transactions, and the sum of transaction outputs must not exceed the sum of inputs.

=== Blocks ===
Blocks record and confirm when and in what sequence transactions enter and are logged in the block chain. Blocks are created by users known as "miners" who use specialized software or equipment designed specifically to create blocks. 

In a cryptocurrency system, miners are incentivized to create blocks to collect two types of rewards: a pre-defined per-block award, and fees offered within the transactions themselves, payable to any miner who successfully confirms the transaction.

=== Decentralization ===
{{expand section|decentralization via blockchains in systems that are not cryptocurrencies|date=March 2016}}<!-- the section seems to only to discuss/explicate decentralization in cryptocurrency uses of blockchain technology.  How does decentralization affect, or how is it used, in other uses of blockchain technology?  Another alternative would be, for now, just renaming the section to be about the material actually explicated here:  "Decentralization in cryptocurrencies" -->
Every [[Node (networking)|node]] in a decentralized cryptocurrency system has at least a partial copy of the block chain. This avoids the need to have a centralized database.{{r|"primer"}} Transactions of the form ''payer X sends Y currency to payee Z'' are broadcast to this network using software applications. Network nodes can validate transactions, add them to their copy and then [[Broadcasting (networking)|broadcast]] these ledger additions to other nodes.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 8}}

Cryptocurrencies use various timestamping schemes, such as [[proof-of-work]], to avoid the need for a trusted third party to timestamp transactions. This prevents users from [[double-spending]] their coins.{{r|kopstein}}

Whereas a conventional [[ledger]] records the transfers of actual [[Banknote|bills]] or [[promissory note]]s that exist apart from it, the block chain is the only place that cryptocurrency can be said to exist, in the form of unspent outputs of transactions.{{r|"Antonopoulos2014"|page=ch. 5}}

==Advantages ==
The core advantages of the block chain architecture include the following:
* The ability for independent nodes to converge on a [[Consensus (computer science)|consensus]] of the latest version of a large data set such as a [[ledger]], even when the nodes are run anonymously, have poor interconnectivity and have operators who are dishonest or malicious (see [[Sybil attack]]).
* The ability for any well-connected node to determine, with reasonable certainty, whether a transaction does or does not exist in the data set (see [[consistency (database systems)|consistency]]).
* The ability for any node that creates a [[Transaction processing|transaction]] to, after a confirmation period, determine with a reasonable level of certainty whether the transaction is valid, able to take place and become final (i.e., that no conflicting transactions were confirmed into the block chain elsewhere that would invalidate the transaction, such as the same currency units "double-spent" somewhere else).
* A prohibitively high cost to attempt to rewrite or alter transaction history.
* Automated [[conflict resolution]] that ensures that [[Write–write conflict|conflicting transactions]] (such as two or more attempts to spend the same balance in different places) never become part of the confirmed data set.

==Debates ==
An ongoing debate disputes whether a private system with verifiers tasked and authorized (permissioned) by a central authority, should still be considered a block chain.{{R|t16}}{{R|t10}}{{R|t11}}{{R|t8}}{{R|t9}}

Proponents of permissioned or private chains argue that the term "blockchain" is rightly applied to any data structure which batches data into blocks which are timestamped and that these blockchains serve as a distributed version of [[multiversion concurrency control]] (MVCC) in databases.{{R|t4}} Just as MVCC prevents two transactions from concurrently modifying a single object in a database, block chains prevent two transactions from spending a single output in a blockchain.

The opponents say that the permissioned systems look like traditional corporate databases, not supporting decentralized verification of the data, and that such systems are not hardened against tampering and revision by its operators.{{R|t11}}{{R|t16}}

== Applications ==
===  Sidechains ===
Sidechains are networks based on the bitcoin protocol that are isolated from the block chain, allowing activity to exist in isolation until confirmation on the block chain, at which point transferability becomes bidirectional.{{R|t5}} Examples:

* Liquid – Exchange sidechain developed by Blockstream{{R|t6}}
* ChromaWay – Sidechain platform for [[colored coins]]
* DIONS – Digital I/O sidechain concept for identity
* tØ (tee-zero) – [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]]-approved sidechain developed by [[Overstock.com]]
* [https://docs.openchain.org Openchain] – NoConsensus NoP2P modular distributed ledger technology for asset management, bidirectional pegging, and chain anchoring (or "sidechaining").

===  Alternative block chains ===
Alternate block chains (altchains) are based on bitcoin technology in concept and/or code.<ref>{{cite web|title=Blockchain|url=http://www.investopedia.com/terms/b/blockchain.asp|website=Investopedia|accessdate=19 March 2016|quote=Based on the Bitcoin protocol, the blockchain database is shared by all nodes participating in a system.}}</ref> These designs generally add functionality to the block chain design. Altchains can provide solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered to be such. Altchains target performance, anonymity, storage and applications such as [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, block chain technology is extending to activities including decentralized applications and collaborative organizations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}} Notable designs include:

* [[Billon standard|Billon]] – Regulated "cryptocash" block chain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15-second block time. Uses Ether as its token.
* LaZooz – decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Coppola, Benmeleh|first1=Gabrielle, Yaacov|title=This Israeli Ride-Sharing App Is the Utopian, Hippie Uber|url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-09-16/this-israeli-ride-sharing-app-is-the-utopian-hippie-uber|website=Bloomberg|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency that can store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Nxt]] – Cryptocurrency financial platform that uses proof of stake to reach consensus for transactions. It has an integrated Asset Exchange, messaging system and marketplace.
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency-based token incorporating [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* Swarm and Koinify – decentralized [[crowdfunding]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Ayral|first1=Sandrine|title=Bitcoin 2.0 Crowdfunding Is Real Crowdfunding|url=http://techcrunch.com/2014/10/17/bitcoin-2-0-crowdfunding-is-real-crowdfunding/|website=Techcrunch|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* Synereo – [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Miller|first1=Carl|title=In 2015 social media companies strengthened their rule. In 2016 they will face rebellion|url=http://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/social-media/12061333/In-2015-social-media-companies-strengthened-their-rule.-In-2016-they-will-face-rebellion.html|website=The Telegraph|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>

=== Trusted timestamp ===
The bitcoin block chain can be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages. Third party application services store messages directly in the block chain, allowing anyone who has the block chain to read the message.<ref>Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin Core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others discouraged embedding large messages in the bitcoin block chain, criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain, recording data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This information can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

=== Other uses ===
[[Cooperative storage cloud]] solutions sometimes employ block chain technology to regulate exchange of data or actual payments for storage space.

Establishing easily provable date-­of­-existence for website pages.  This opens up the

possibility that internet search engines can offer verifiable age as a search criteria, and also that

claimed dates for website revision histories can be established.

Verifying the authenticity of important legal documents that might be subjected to tampering

(forgery) or accusations of tampering or backdating.  For example, if one party to a contract

alters its copy, and then sues the other party while claiming that the altered version is the true

copy, there may be a non­-negligible risk that a court proceeding could make an incorrect

determination.  However, had the original contract been entered into a properly-­implemented

block chain scheme, and the block chain entry attested to by the contracting parties, the authentic

copy of the contract could be easily identified by a neutral third party at the time of the dispute.

=== Prior Invention ===
Block chaining had actually been invented prior to the appearance of the Nakamoto paper, which

was published 31 October 2008.  US Patent No. 7,904,450, the first in a series of patents on the

Public Electronic Document Dating List™ (PEDDaL®) system, was filed more than six months

prior to the publication of the Nakamoto paper, on 25 April 2008 and publicly disclosed as US

Patent Application Publication No. 2009/0100041 on 16 April 2009.

Alternative block chains (altchains) are based on bitcoin technology in concept and/or code.<ref>{{cite web|title=Blockchain|url=http://www.investopedia.com/terms/b/blockchain.asp|website=Investopedia|accessdate=19 March 2016|quote=Based on the Bitcoin protocol, the blockchain database is shared by all nodes participating in a system.}}</ref> These designs generally add functionality to the block chain design. Altchains can provide solutions including other digital currencies, although tokens used in these designs are not always considered to be such. Altchains target performance, anonymity, storage and applications such as [[smart contract]]s.<ref>{{cite web|title=Why Bitcoin may herald a new era in finance|url=http://www.economistinsights.com/technology-innovation/analysis/money-no-middleman/tab/1|website=www.economistinsights.com|publisher=The Economist Group|accessdate=9 June 2015}}</ref> Starting with a strong focus on financial applications, block chain technology is extending to activities including decentralized applications and collaborative organizations that eliminate a middleman.<ref>{{cite web|title=Primavera De Filippi: From competition to cooperation|url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aYOPcHRO3tc|publisher=TEDxCambridge|accessdate=8 October 2015}}</ref>{{Primary source inline|date=November 2015}} Notable designs include:

* [[Billon standard|Billon]] – Regulated "cryptocash" block chain solution as digital cash for governmental fiat currencies
* [[Ethereum]] – Network supporting storage of [[turing-complete]] [[smart contract]]s at specified addresses with a 15-second block time. Uses Ether as its token.
* LaZooz – decentralized [[Real-time ridesharing|real-time ride sharing]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Coppola, Benmeleh|first1=Gabrielle, Yaacov|title=This Israeli Ride-Sharing App Is the Utopian, Hippie Uber|url=http://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-09-16/this-israeli-ride-sharing-app-is-the-utopian-hippie-uber|website=Bloomberg|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* [[Namecoin]] – Digital currency that can store [[data]] within a chain
* [[Nxt]] – Cryptocurrency financial platform that uses proof of stake to reach consensus for transactions. It has an integrated Asset Exchange, messaging system and marketplace.
* [[Mastercoin]] – Metaprotocol with the ability to process various transactions and sub-currencies
* [[Peercoin]] – Cryptocurrency-based token incorporating [[proof of stake]] in its consensus model
* Swarm and Koinify – decentralized [[crowdfunding]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Ayral|first1=Sandrine|title=Bitcoin 2.0 Crowdfunding Is Real Crowdfunding|url=http://techcrunch.com/2014/10/17/bitcoin-2-0-crowdfunding-is-real-crowdfunding/|website=Techcrunch|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>
* Synereo – [[Comparison of synchronous and asynchronous signalling|synchronous and asynchronous communication]]<ref>{{cite web|last1=Miller|first1=Carl|title=In 2015 social media companies strengthened their rule. In 2016 they will face rebellion|url=http://www.telegraph.co.uk/technology/social-media/12061333/In-2015-social-media-companies-strengthened-their-rule.-In-2016-they-will-face-rebellion.html|website=The Telegraph|accessdate=27 January 2016}}</ref>

=== Trusted timestamp ===
The bitcoin block chain can be used as a [[trusted timestamp]] for arbitrary messages. Third party application services store messages directly in the block chain, allowing anyone who has the block chain to read the message.<ref>Aaron van Wirdum.
[http://www.coindesk.com/students-aims-boost-free-speech-bitcoin-messaging-app/ "Student Aims to Boost Free Speech with Bitcoin Messaging App"].
</ref><ref>Ken Shirriff.
[http://www.righto.com/2014/02/ascii-bernanke-wikileaks-photographs.html "Hidden surprises in the Bitcoin blockchain and how they are stored: Nelson Mandela, Wikileaks, photos, and Python software"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoinstrings.com/ "Bitcoin Strings: blockchain in words"]
</ref><ref>[http://www.cryptograffiti.info/ "CryptoGraffiti"].
</ref> Bitcoin Core developer [[Mike Hearn]] among others discouraged embedding large messages in the bitcoin block chain, criticizing it as "[[Software bloat|bloat]]".<ref>Daniel Cawrey.
[http://www.coindesk.com/new-forms-spam-bloat-bitcoins-block-chain/ "Why New Forms of Spam Could Bloat Bitcoin's Block Chain"].
</ref><ref>[http://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/questions/2631/what-are-the-key-differences-between-different-ways-of-embedding-messages-in-the "What are the key differences between different ways of embedding messages in the blockchain?"].
</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.
[http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/06/26/bitcoin_unbloating_blockchain/ "Bitcoin, schmitcoin. Let's play piggyback on the blockchain"].
</ref>

Other applications store a hash value in the block chain, recording data existence and confirming data integrity without revealing data and without bloating the block chain.<ref>[https://proofofexistence.com/about "What is proof of existence?"].</ref><ref>Danny Bradbury.[http://www.coindesk.com/developers-battle-bitcoin-block-chain/ "Developers Battle Over Bitcoin Block Chain"].</ref> This information can be used to implement "[[colored coins]]" or side chains to support functionality such as [[smart contract]]s.

=== Other uses ===
[[Cooperative storage cloud]] solutions sometimes employ block chain technology to regulate exchange of data or actual payments for storage space.

=== Prior Invention ===
Block chaining had actually been invented prior to the appearance of the Nakamoto paper, which

was published 31 October 2008.  US Patent No. 7,904,450, the first in a series of patents on the

Public Electronic Document Dating List™ (PEDDaL®) system, was filed more than six months

prior to the publication of the Nakamoto paper, on 25 April 2008 and publicly disclosed as US

Patent Application Publication No. 2009/0100041 on 16 April 2009. {{R|t38}}{{R|t39}}
